sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A county health report compared three income groups on self-rated health and preventable hospitalizations. In the lowest-income group, 49% rated their health as fair or poor and there were 31 preventable hospitalizations per 1,000 adults. In the middle-income group, the values were 32% and 19 per 1,000. In the highest-income group, the values were 18% and 9 per 1,000. The report noted that lower-income neighborhoods also had fewer primary-care clinics, more shift-work schedules, and longer travel times to pharmacies.
questions.stem: Which group had the highest preventable hospitalization rate?

choices.D: All groups had the same rate

explanations.A: Correct. The lowest-income group had 31 preventable hospitalizations per 1,000 adults, compared with 19 and 9 in the middle- and highest-income groups. The figure and passage both show a clear income gradient.
explanations.B: The middle-income group was intermediate rather than highest.
explanations.C: The highest-income group had the lowest preventable hospitalization rate.
explanations.D: The rates differ substantially across income groups.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Self-rated fair/poor health and preventable hospitalizations by income group.

questions.stem: The overall pattern best illustrates which concept?

explanations.C: Correct. Health outcomes worsen stepwise as income decreases, which is a socioeconomic health gradient. The passage ties that pattern to neighborhood resources, work schedules, and pharmacy access rather than only individual choices.
explanations.A: Object permanence is a developmental milestone and does not explain population health gradients.
explanations.B: The mere-exposure effect concerns liking through familiarity, not income-linked health outcomes.
explanations.D: Role exit is leaving a social role, not the pattern shown here.

questions.stem: Why should the report not conclude that income alone directly caused every hospitalization?

choices.B: Income is associated with other conditions such as clinic access, work schedules, and pharmacy distance
choices.C: Self-rated health is impossible to collect
choices.D: The highest-income group had no health problems

explanations.B: Correct. Income groups differ on multiple related social conditions. These factors may mediate or confound the relationship, so the report should describe a broad social gradient rather than a single isolated causal pathway.
explanations.A: The passage reports hospitalization rates per 1,000 adults.
explanations.C: Self-rated health is a common survey measure even though it is subjective.
explanations.D: The highest-income group still had some poor health ratings and hospitalizations.

questions.stem: A policy response most consistent with the passage would be what?

choices.D: Improve primary-care and pharmacy access in lower-income neighborhoods
choices.A: Tell patients that access barriers are purely personal failings
choices.B: Reduce clinic hours in neighborhoods with shift workers
choices.C: Focus only on hospital care after preventable conditions worsen

explanations.D: Correct. The report identifies fewer clinics, longer pharmacy travel, and shift-work constraints. Improving access to preventive and primary care directly targets the conditions linked to preventable hospitalizations.
explanations.A: The passage frames barriers as structural, not purely personal.
explanations.B: Reducing clinic hours would worsen access for shift workers.
explanations.C: Preventable hospitalizations point to the need for earlier care, not only downstream hospital treatment.

questions.stem: Which additional analysis would best test whether clinic access mediates the income-health relationship?

choices.B: Examine whether the income gap shrinks after accounting for distance to primary-care clinics
choices.A: Compare favorite television shows across income groups
choices.C: Remove hospitalization data from the report
choices.D: Measure only genetic ancestry and ignore neighborhood resources

explanations.B: Correct. If clinic distance partly explains the income gradient, statistically accounting for clinic access should reduce the income-health association. That would support mediation by access to care.
explanations.A: Television preference does not test the healthcare-access pathway.
explanations.C: Removing hospitalization data would make it harder to analyze the outcome of interest.
explanations.D: Genetic ancestry alone would not test the neighborhood-resource mechanism described.

questions.passage: A diabetes education program mapped social support among 140 adults managing type 2 diabetes. Participants who reported at least two supportive contacts for medication reminders and meal planning were more likely to attend classes and bring glucose logs to visits. Participants with fewer supportive contacts often described managing the illness alone and feeling embarrassed to discuss diet changes with family. Educators introduced optional family sessions so relatives could learn how to provide reminders without criticism or blame.
questions.stem: Which participants showed better engagement with diabetes management?

choices.A: Those with at least two supportive contacts

explanations.A: Correct. The passage states that participants with at least two supportive contacts were more likely to attend classes and bring glucose logs. These are direct markers of engagement with the diabetes program.
explanations.B: Managing entirely alone was associated with more difficulty, not better engagement.
explanations.C: Embarrassment about diet discussion was reported among those with fewer supports.
explanations.D: No class attendance would be the opposite of the engagement described.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Program engagement measures by number of supportive diabetes-management contacts.

questions.stem: Medication reminders and meal-planning help are examples of what?

explanations.C: Correct. Reminders provide practical assistance, and meal-planning help can provide information and structure. Both are concrete forms of support that can improve chronic-disease management.
explanations.A: The bystander effect involves reduced helping in groups during emergencies, not ongoing diabetes support.
explanations.B: Sensory adaptation is unrelated to medication reminders or meal planning.
explanations.D: Cognitive dissonance may affect beliefs, but it is not the main support process described.

questions.stem: Why did the program include family sessions?

choices.D: To make support more effective and less blaming
choices.A: To remove patients from all decision-making
choices.B: To replace medication with family opinions
choices.C: To prevent relatives from learning about diabetes

explanations.D: Correct. The passage says relatives were taught to provide reminders without criticism or blame. That targets the quality of support, not just the amount, because unsupportive or shaming reminders could reduce engagement.
explanations.A: The program supports patient management rather than removing patient agency.
explanations.B: Family education complements medical management and does not replace medication.
explanations.C: The goal is explicitly to help relatives learn about diabetes support.

questions.stem: A patient embarrassed to discuss diet changes may be experiencing what barrier?

choices.A: Stigma or discomfort within close social relationships

explanations.A: Correct. Embarrassment about discussing diet with family suggests that social judgment or discomfort can interfere with disease management. This is a social barrier, not simply a lack of medical instructions.
explanations.B: Embarrassment would likely reduce, not improve, confidence in communicating needs.
explanations.C: The problem arises within social relationships, so social context is present.
explanations.D: The barrier concerns communication and stigma, not visual perception.

choices.C: Social support is associated with better engagement, but motivation or illness severity could also differ between groups
choices.A: Supportive contacts prove glucose control improves for every patient
choices.B: Family sessions are harmful because they involve relatives
choices.D: Support has no possible relevance to chronic illness

explanations.C: Correct. The study maps support and engagement but does not randomly assign support networks. People with more support may differ in motivation, resources, or illness complexity, so the association should be interpreted carefully.
explanations.A: The passage describes engagement outcomes, not guaranteed glucose improvement for every patient.
explanations.B: Family sessions are introduced to improve support quality, not shown to be harmful.
explanations.D: The passage provides several ways support can matter for chronic illness management.
